heb.paragraph: The noun derives from the same root as 'call out' (qara). In 1:2 God said 'call out against' (qera aleiha); now in 3:2 he says 'proclaim the message I give you' (qera et haqqeriʾah). The shift from 'against' to 'the proclamation' subtly opens the possibility that the message is not merely judgment but opportunity.

The verb hpk can mean either 'overturn/destroy' (as with Sodom, Gen 19:21, 25) or 'transform/change.' Jonah means destruction; God may intend transformation. The ambiguity is theologically deliberate: Nineveh is indeed 'overturned' — not by destruction but by repentance.

panels.ctx: The recommission in 3:1-2 closely parallels the original commission in 1:1-2, creating a structural restart. The phrase 'a second time' (shenit) emphasizes divine persistence: God does not abandon his purpose or his prophet. Jonah's five-word sermon ('Forty more days and Nineveh will be overturned') is the shortest prophetic oracle in the Hebrew Bible. Its brevity may reflect Jonah's reluctance: he delivers the minimum possible message with no call to repentance. Yet this bare oracle achieves what no other prophetic preaching in the OT accomplishes — the total repentance of an entire city.

cross.note: The 'overturning' of Sodom uses the same Hebrew verb (hpk). Nineveh faces the same threat but receives a different outcome.
The prophetic principle: if a nation repents, God will relent from the disaster he planned. This is the theological framework behind Nineveh's reprieve.
Jesus declares that the men of Nineveh will rise at the judgment and condemn 'this generation' because they repented at Jonah's preaching.

notes.note: God gave Jonah a second chance, demonstrating the same mercy toward his prophet that he was about to demonstrate toward Nineveh. The recommission uses identical language to 1:1-2, confirming that God's purpose has not changed.

Nineveh was 'a very large city — a visit required three days.' Archaeological evidence confirms that greater Nineveh, including its suburbs, covered approximately sixty miles in circumference. This was the largest city in the ancient world at its peak.

Forty days is a period of testing throughout Scripture (the flood, Moses on Sinai, Israel in the wilderness, Jesus' temptation). The number signals a probationary period within which repentance is possible.

Calvin emphasizes the persistence of divine grace: God does not discard the disobedient prophet but renews the call. This pattern — fall, discipline, restoration, recommission — is central to the Reformed understanding of sanctification.

Calvin notes that Jonah's preaching contained no explicit invitation to repent. The bare announcement of judgment was sufficient because the Spirit of God worked inwardly on the Ninevites' hearts. The efficacy of preaching depends not on the preacher's eloquence but on God's sovereign work.

The phrase 'proclaim to it the message I give you' (qera eleiha et haqqeriʾah asher anokhi dover eleiha) is more specific than the original commission, which simply said 'cry against it.' The shift may indicate that God is now providing the content of the message, constraining Jonah's options.

The LXX reads 'three days' instead of 'forty days,' possibly reflecting a different Hebrew Vorlage or a scribal assimilation to the three-day motif that runs through the book. The MT's 'forty days' is generally preferred as the more difficult reading.

Stuart observes that the recommission is virtually verbatim from 1:1-2, a literary device signaling that God's purpose is immutable even when his messenger fails. The narrator's description of Nineveh as 'a city great to God' (ir gedolah l-elohim) is unique in the Hebrew Bible and may be a superlative ('an exceedingly great city') or may carry theological weight: Nineveh matters to God.

Stuart analyzes the five-word oracle (arbaʿim yom weninewe nehpakhet) as deliberately ambiguous. The verb hpk ('overturn') functions as the pivot of the entire book: will Nineveh be overturned like Sodom or transformed like a penitent? The forty-day period provides the narrative space for the Ninevites to decide.

sections.header: Verses 5-10 — "Who Knows? God May Yet Relent"

heb.gloss: to relent / be sorry / change one's mind
heb.paragraph: This is the key theological verb of the chapter. It does not imply that God made a mistake but that he responds to changed human circumstances. The prophetic principle of conditional judgment (Jer 18:7-8) governs: when people turn, God turns. The same verb describes what Jonah feared would happen (4:2).

The king's question 'Who knows?' (v. 9) expresses genuine uncertainty about divine response. It is not presumption but humble hope. The same phrase appears in Joel 2:14 and 2 Sam 12:22 — each time as an act of faith in the face of judgment.

panels.ctx: The Ninevite response is without parallel in the Hebrew Bible. An entire city — from king to commoner to animals — repents in sackcloth and fasting. The king's decree mandates universal mourning and the cessation of violence and evil. The narrator then states that God 'saw what they did and how they turned from their evil ways' and relented from the disaster he had threatened. This is the largest scale conversion event in the OT, achieved by the shortest sermon, preached by the most reluctant prophet. The theological implications are enormous: if YHWH's mercy extends to the worst of the pagan world, no one is beyond its reach.

cross.note: 'Who knows? He may turn and relent' — the same theological logic and nearly identical phrasing as the king of Nineveh's hope.
David's 'Who knows? The LORD may be gracious to me' after the death sentence on his child — the same posture of humble hope before sovereign mercy.
Jesus contrasts Nineveh's repentance with Israel's hardness: 'The men of Nineveh will stand up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it.'

notes.note: The Ninevites believed God — not Jonah but God. Faith came through the proclaimed word, however minimal. The response was immediate and universal: fasting and sackcloth from the greatest to the least.

The king's decree extended even to animals, reflecting the ANE practice of including livestock in communal mourning rites. The decree demanded not just ritual mourning but moral reform: 'Let everyone call urgently on God and give up their evil ways and their violence.'

God 'relented' does not mean God changed his mind in the sense of reversing a mistake. It means God responded appropriately to changed circumstances. His character is unchanging; his dealings with people are responsive to their choices.

Calvin marvels at the speed and totality of Nineveh's repentance, attributing it entirely to the work of the Holy Spirit. The bare word of judgment, preached without persuasion or call to repentance, accomplished what Israel's prophets failed to achieve over centuries.

Calvin treats God's relenting as consistent with divine immutability. God does not change in his being or purpose; rather, the conditional nature of prophetic threat means that repentance activates the mercy that was always latent in the warning.

The phrase 'believed God' (heʾeminu belohim) uses the same verb (aman) applied to Abraham's faith in Gen 15:6. The narrator draws an implicit parallel between the patriarch's faith and Nineveh's faith.

The verb niham ('relent/change one's mind') here is the Niphal of nhm. In prophetic theology, divine 'relenting' is the appropriate response to human repentance. It does not indicate caprice but covenantal responsiveness.

Stuart argues that the Ninevite response, while extraordinary, is historically plausible given what we know of Mesopotamian penitential practices. Communal fasting, sackcloth, and royal proclamations of mourning are well-attested in Assyrian texts. The inclusion of animals (v. 8) has parallels in Persian royal mourning practices (cf. Herodotus 9.24). The king's 'Who knows?' is not skepticism but the appropriate human posture before sovereign mercy.

Stuart reads God's relenting as the fulfillment of the Jeremiah 18 principle: prophetic threats of judgment are implicitly conditional. When the condition changes (repentance), the consequence changes (mercy). This is not divine inconsistency but divine integrity — God is faithful to his own character, which is merciful.

rows.label: vv.1-4
rows.text: The Recommission and the Five-Word Sermon

rows.label: vv.5-10
rows.text: The Great Repentance: King to Cattle

lit.note: Chapter 3 parallels chapter 1 in structure: divine command (vv. 1-2 // 1:1-2), Jonah's response (v. 3 // 1:3), and the response of pagans (vv. 5-10 // 1:14-16). Both chapters end with pagans worshipping YHWH, while Jonah remains spiritually resistant.

themes.note: Mercy peaks at 9: this is the chapter where God relents from judgment. Mission at 9: the most successful evangelistic campaign in the OT. Faith at 8: Nineveh 'believed God.' Prophecy at 8: the shortest oracle in the Bible achieves the greatest response.

ppl.text: Jonah's five-word sermon ('Forty more days and Nineveh will be overturned') is the shortest prophetic oracle in the Hebrew Bible…

ppl.name: Jesus
ppl.text: Forty days is a period of testing throughout Scripture (the flood, Moses on Sinai, Israel in the wilderness, Jesus' temptation)…

translations.text: But let people and animals be covered with sackcloth. Let everyone call urgently on God. Let them give up their evil ways and their violence.
but let man and beast be covered with sackcloth, and let them call out mightily to God. Let everyone turn from his evil way and from the violence that is in his hands.
When Jonah’s warning reached the king of Nineveh, he rose from his throne, took off his royal robes, covered himself with sackcloth and sat down in the dust.
The word reached the king of Nineveh, and he arose from his throne, removed his robe, covered himself with sackcloth, and sat in ashes.
